concepts.title: Introduction
concepts.definition: This chapter introduces the number system, building from natural numbers to rational and irrational numbers, culminating in real numbers.

concepts.title: Irrational Numbers
concepts.definition: A number 's' is called irrational if it cannot be written in the form p/q, where p and q are integers and q ≠ 0. Irrational numbers have non-terminating non-recurring decimal expansions.

concepts.title: Real Numbers and their Decimal Expansions
concepts.definition: The decimal expansion of a rational number is either terminating or non-terminating recurring. The decimal expansion of an irrational number is non-terminating non-recurring. All rational and irrational numbers together make up the collection of real numbers.

concepts.title: Operations on Real Numbers
concepts.definition: Real numbers follow commutative, associative, and distributive laws. When we add, subtract, multiply or divide rational and irrational numbers, specific patterns emerge.

concepts.title: Laws of Exponents for Real Numbers
concepts.definition: Laws of exponents extend to real numbers with rational exponents, allowing us to perform operations with powers and roots systematically.

concepts.title: Introduction
concepts.definition: A polynomial is an algebraic expression in which the exponents of all variables are whole numbers. Polynomials form the foundation for algebraic manipulation.

concepts.title: Polynomials in One Variable
concepts.definition: A polynomial in one variable x of degree n is an expression of the form: aₙx^n + aₙ₋₁x^(n-1) + ... + a₁x + a₀, where a₀, a₁, ..., aₙ are constants and aₙ ≠ 0.

concepts.title: Zeroes of a Polynomial
concepts.definition: A zero of a polynomial p(x) is a number c such that p(c) = 0. Every linear polynomial has exactly one zero.
formulae: If p(x) = ax + b, then zero is x = -b/a
examples: p(x) = x - 5 has zero at x = 5

concepts.title: Factorisation of Polynomials
concepts.definition: Remainder Theorem: If polynomial p(x) is divided by (x - a), the remainder is p(a). Factor Theorem: (x - a) is a factor of p(x) if and only if p(a) = 0.
formulae: If p(a) = 0, then (x - a) is a factor
examples: If p(x) = x² - 5x + 6 and p(2) = 0, then (x - 2) is a factor
p(x) = x³ - 1 has (x - 1) as factor since p(1) = 0

concepts.title: Algebraic Identities
concepts.definition: Algebraic identities are equations that are true for all values of the variables. They help in factorization and expansion of algebraic expressions.

concepts.title: Introduction
concepts.definition: Coordinate geometry (or analytical geometry) is the study of geometry using coordinate systems. It provides a connection between algebra and geometry through graphs of equations.

concepts.title: Cartesian System
concepts.definition: The Cartesian system uses two perpendicular number lines (axes) intersecting at the origin. Any point in the plane can be represented by an ordered pair (x, y), where x is the x-coordinate and y is the y-coordinate.

concepts.title: Introduction
concepts.definition: Linear equations in two variables arise naturally in many real-world situations where two quantities are related linearly.
examples: Cost = 5x + 3y where x is quantity of item A and y is quantity of item B

concepts.title: Linear Equations
concepts.definition: An equation of the form ax + by + c = 0, where a, b, c are real numbers and a, b are not both zero, is called a linear equation in two variables x and y.

concepts.title: Solution of a Linear Equation
concepts.definition: A solution of a linear equation in two variables is a pair of values (x, y) that makes the equation true when substituted.

concepts.title: Introduction
concepts.definition: Euclid, a Greek mathematician around 300 BC, systematized geometry by developing it from a small set of basic assumptions called axioms and postulates.
examples: Axiom: The whole is greater than the part
Postulate: All right angles are equal

concepts.title: Euclid's Definitions, Axioms and Postulates
concepts.definition: Euclid's axioms are self-evident universal truths. Postulates are geometric truths. Together they form the foundation of Euclidean geometry.
examples: Axiom: Things equal to the same thing are equal to one another
Postulate: A straight line may be drawn from any point to any other point
Postulate: A circle may be described with any center and any radius

concepts.title: Introduction
concepts.definition: Lines and angles form the basic elements of geometry. Understanding their properties and relationships is fundamental to geometric reasoning.

concepts.title: Basic Terms and Definitions
concepts.definition: Basic geometric terms: A ray is part of a line with one endpoint. A line segment is part of a line with two endpoints. An angle is formed by two rays sharing a common endpoint (vertex).
examples: Ray AB starts at A and goes through B
Line segment PQ has endpoints P and Q
Angle ABC has vertex at B

concepts.title: Intersecting Lines and Non-intersecting Lines
concepts.definition: Two lines are intersecting if they meet at a point. Two lines are parallel if they never meet, no matter how far they are extended.
examples: Lines x + y = 5 and x - y = 3 intersect at (4, 1)
Lines y = 2x + 3 and y = 2x - 1 are parallel

concepts.title: Pairs of Angles
concepts.definition: Complementary angles sum to 90°. Supplementary angles sum to 180°. Adjacent angles share a common vertex and arm. Vertically opposite angles are equal.
examples: When two lines intersect, opposite angles are equal

concepts.title: Lines Parallel to the Same Line
concepts.definition: If two lines are parallel to the same line, they are parallel to each other. This is a fundamental property of parallel lines.
examples: If line l ∥ m and line m ∥ n, then line l ∥ n

concepts.title: Introduction
concepts.definition: A triangle is a closed figure with three sides and three angles. Triangles have important properties regarding congruence and similarity.

concepts.title: Congruence of Triangles
concepts.definition: Two triangles are congruent if all corresponding sides and angles are equal. Congruent triangles have the same shape and size.
examples: △ABC ≅ △PQR means AB = PQ, BC = QR, CA = RP and all angles equal

concepts.title: Criteria for Congruence of Triangles
concepts.definition: Congruence criteria: SSS (three sides), SAS (two sides and included angle), ASA (two angles and included side), RHS (right angle, hypotenuse, and one side).
formulae: SSS: AB = PQ, BC = QR, CA = RP
examples: If AB = 5, BC = 6, CA = 7 and PQ = 5, QR = 6, RP = 7, then △ABC ≅ △PQR by SSS

concepts.title: Some Properties of a Triangle
concepts.definition: In a triangle, angles opposite to equal sides are equal, and sides opposite to equal angles are equal.
examples: In isosceles triangle with AB = AC, we have ∠B = ∠C

concepts.title: Some More Criteria for Congruence of Triangles
concepts.definition: AAS (Angle-Angle-Side) criterion: If two angles and one non-included side of one triangle are equal to corresponding parts of another triangle, the triangles are congruent.
examples: If ∠A = 60°, ∠B = 80°, BC = 5 in △ABC and same in △PQR, then △ABC ≅ △PQR

concepts.title: Properties of a Parallelogram
concepts.definition: A parallelogram is a quadrilateral with opposite sides parallel. Properties: opposite sides are equal, opposite angles are equal, diagonals bisect each other.
examples: In parallelogram ABCD: AB ∥ DC, AD ∥ BC, AB = DC, AD = BC, ∠A = ∠C, ∠B = ∠D

concepts.title: The Mid-point Theorem
concepts.definition: The Mid-point Theorem states that the line segment joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half its length.
formulae: If D, E are midpoints of AB, AC respectively, then DE ∥ BC and DE = (1/2)BC
examples: In △ABC, if D is midpoint of AB and E is midpoint of AC, then DE = (1/2)BC

concepts.title: Angle Subtended by a Chord at a Point
concepts.definition: A chord is a line segment joining two points on a circle. Equal chords of a circle subtend equal angles at the center.
examples: If chord AB = chord CD, then ∠AOB = ∠COD where O is center

concepts.title: Perpendicular from the Centre to a Chord
concepts.definition: The perpendicular from the center of a circle to a chord bisects the chord.
examples: If OM ⊥ AB where O is center and AB is chord, then AM = MB

concepts.title: Equal Chords and their Distances from the Centre
concepts.definition: Equal chords of a circle are equidistant from the center. Conversely, chords equidistant from center are equal.
examples: If AB = CD, then OM = ON where M, N are feet of perpendiculars from O to AB, CD

concepts.title: Angle Subtended by an Arc of a Circle
concepts.definition: An arc is a portion of the circumference of a circle. Equal arcs of a circle subtend equal angles at the center.
examples: If arc AB = arc CD, then ∠AOB = ∠COD

concepts.title: Cyclic Quadrilaterals
concepts.definition: A cyclic quadrilateral is a quadrilateral whose all vertices lie on a circle. The sum of opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is 180°.
examples: In cyclic quadrilateral ABCD, if ∠A = 110°, then ∠C = 70°

concepts.title: Area of a Triangle – by Heron's Formula
concepts.definition: Heron's formula allows calculation of the area of a triangle when all three sides are known. The semi-perimeter s is half the perimeter.
examples: For triangle with sides 3, 4, 5: s = 6, Area = √[6×3×2×1] = 6 square units

concepts.title: Surface Area of a Right Circular Cone
concepts.definition: A right circular cone has a circular base and a vertex directly above the center. The slant height l is the distance from vertex to any point on the circular edge of base.
examples: Cone with r = 3, h = 4: l = 5, CSA = 15π, TSA = 24π

concepts.title: Surface Area of a Sphere
concepts.definition: A sphere is a three-dimensional object where all points on the surface are equidistant from the center.
examples: Sphere with radius 7 cm: Surface area = 4π(7)² = 196π cm²

concepts.title: Volume of a Right Circular Cone
concepts.definition: The volume of a right circular cone is one-third the volume of a cylinder with the same base radius and height.
examples: Cone with r = 3 cm, h = 7 cm: Volume = (1/3)π(3)²(7) = 21π cm³

concepts.title: Volume of a Sphere
concepts.definition: The volume of a sphere is the space occupied by it in three dimensions.
examples: Sphere with radius 3 cm: Volume = (4/3)π(3)³ = 36π cm³

concepts.title: Graphical Representation of Data
concepts.definition: Graphical representation of data helps in visual understanding of information. Common methods include bar graphs, histograms, frequency polygons, and pie charts.
